Ps 23; Ezek 34:23-24: "I will place over them one shepherd, my servant David") invokes both the Davidic-pastoral and the Ezekiel-messianic shepherd traditions, positioning the benediction within the full scope of the OT's messianic hope.
13.21: "Equip you with everything good for doing his will" (katartisai hymas en panti agathō) uses the katartizō verb that appears in Ephesians 4:12 for the "equipping of the saints" for ministry, and in the Gospel accounts of mending fishing nets (Matt 4:21). The word carries the sense of fitting something out completely for its purpose — the community is fitted for will-doing as a ship is fitted out for a voyage. The phrase "may he work in us what is pleasing to him, through Jesus Christ" reflects the theological conviction shared with Paul (Phil 2:13: "it is God who works in you to will and to act according to his good purpose") that the community's own will-doing is itself a divine act within them. The benediction closes the entire letter's argument by attributing both the community's obedience and its acceptance to the God who simultaneously commands and enables.
13.22: "Bear with my word of exhortation" (tou logou tēs paraklēseōs) provides the letter's own generic self-identification: a logos tēs paraklēseōs (a word of encouragement or exhortation). This phrase appears elsewhere in Acts 13:15, where the synagogue ruler in Pisidian Antioch invites Paul and Barnabas: "Brothers, if you have a word of exhortation for the people, please speak." The logos paraklēseōs was a recognized genre of synagogue homily — a sermon that encouraged, exhorted, and applied Scripture to the community's situation. Hebrews' own self-identification as this genre positions it within the tradition of Jewish-Christian synagogue preaching rather than purely epistolary form — it is a sermon that has been written down and sent, not merely a letter with theological content.
13.23: The mention of "our brother Timothy" who has been released is one of the few personal details in the letter, connecting it to the Pauline circle. Timothy is mentioned in multiple Pauline letters (1-2 Timothy, Phil 1:1, Col 1:1, Philemon 1, 1-2 Thess) as Paul's closest traveling companion and representative. If this is the same Timothy (and the Greek suggests familiarity — "our Timothy" — that would connect the author and recipients to the same apostolic network), his release from some form of custody is significant. Acts 16:3 introduced Timothy; the Pastoral Letters show him in leadership roles in Ephesus and elsewhere. The mention of his release and the conditional plan for a joint visit ("if he arrives soon, I will come with him") grounds the theological treatise in the practical realities of first-century Christian travel and community networks.
13.24: "Those from Italy send you their greetings" (hoi apo tēs Italias) is one of the letter's key hints about either authorship or destination. The phrase can be read two ways: (a) the author and Italian Christians are writing from Italy to a community elsewhere — "those from Italy" being Italian Christians currently with the author; or (b) Italian expatriates living where the letter was written are sending greetings back to their home community. If reading (b), the letter is destined for Rome or another Italian community. The Roman hypothesis for the letter's destination is supported by Clement of Rome's apparent knowledge of Hebrews (1 Clement, c. 96 CE, appears to cite Hebrews several times), suggesting it was known in Rome very early. The salutation reflects the normal closing practice of Pauline letters (Rom 16:21-23; Phil 4:21-22; Col 4:10-14).
13.25: "Grace be with you all" (hē charis meta pantōn hymōn) is the minimal Pauline benediction form. Paul's letters typically close with some form of this grace-benediction (Rom 16:20; 1 Cor 16:23; 2 Cor 13:14; Gal 6:18; Phil 4:23; Col 4:18; 1 Thess 5:28; 2 Thess 3:18). The brevity of Hebrews' closing benediction (compared to 2 Cor 13:14's full trinitarian form) matches the simpler forms of the shorter letters. The word charis (grace) as the closing word gathers up the letter's entire theological argument: the community has access to God, endurance in trial, and the community obligations of ch. 13 — all ultimately grounded in the grace of the God who made the eternal covenant in Christ's blood and who equips the community for every good work (v21).
